tag: 30 min · a letter from the you five years out
title: The Five-Year Letter
intro: Future-you is not a fantasy. Future-you is a specific person, built by the choices you make in the next 1800 days. This exercise gives that person a voice and lets them talk to you — about what they wish you'd done sooner, what you should stop worrying about, and what mattered more than you thought.

quote.text: Most of what we will regret in five years, we can see from here if we sit still long enough.

deliverable: Two short letters: one from future-you to current-you, one from current-you back. Kept in the journal, re-read when decisions get noisy.

exercises.title: The Letter From Future-You
exercises.sub: 20 min · write as the person you'll be in 5 years, looking back at today
prompt: It is five years from today. Write a letter to yourself as you are right now. Not a fantasy version — a realistic, specific version of you that made the choices you would respect. What do they want you to hear?

Cover at least: (1) one thing you are worrying about now that didn't matter, (2) one thing you are NOT worrying about that should have been a bigger deal, (3) one decision you made in the next 12 months that compounded well, (4) one thing you wish you'd stopped sooner, (5) the sentence future-you most wants current-you to sit with.

guidance.title: Use second person throughout
guidance.body: 'You are spending too much energy on...' rather than 'I was spending.' The second person makes it feel like a letter, not a self-reflection. Letters land harder than reflections.

guidance.title: Be specific to your actual life
guidance.body: Generic advice from a generic future-self is worthless. Name the actual people, the actual projects, the actual worries currently eating your week. Future-you is specific or they're not speaking.

guidance.title: Include one thing that's a little brutal
guidance.body: Not cruel. But honest in a way that current-you would slightly resist hearing. The letters that change anything always include at least one sentence that makes you uncomfortable while writing it.

exercises.watch_out: Writing the letter as aspirational fantasy (palaces, yachts, fame). The useful version is the one from a realistic, grounded future-you who made good ordinary decisions — not the fantasy version. The fantasy letter doesn't change behavior. The realistic one does.

exercises.title: The Letter Back
exercises.sub: 10 min · respond, as you are now, to what future-you said
prompt: Write a short letter back. What did you hear? What landed? What are you going to do differently this week because of what they said? One concrete action — no more, no less.
Close with: 'I will see you in five years. Here is what I promise to have done by then:' — and name one thing. One.

guidance.title: One promise, not a list
guidance.body: The value of the promise is in its singularity. A list of five dilutes each one. A single promise, made to a specific future-you, is the kind of commitment that actually holds.

exercises.tip: Re-read both letters on the first of every month. Not to judge progress — just to let future-you stay present in the decisions of the coming week.
